FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study participants; longitudinal fMRI design with baseline and follow-up; ages 9-10 at baseline

FT_IC5.evidence: Published in Journal of Medical Internet Research, 2026; peer-reviewed journal

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization alone fails IC2 per binding clarifications (§2.4). No phone/SM-specific IV variable (e.g., time on phone, platform use, texting). Cyberbullying is a peer-interaction exposure, not a phone/SM-specific behavioral measure. Other side is neural activation and mental health outcomes, which are individual-trait, but IC2 requires the OTHER side to be individual-trait when SM/phone is IV—here, SM/phone is not present at all.

FT_EC7_reason: IC2 not met; EC7 evaluation deferred
notes: Paper examines neural correlates of cyberbullying victimization. Cyberbullying is not phone/SM-specific; the paper does not measure smartphone or social media use as a predictor. While the outcome (neural activation, mental health) is individual-trait-level, the exposure is cyberbullying victimization without explicit phone/SM operationalization.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; N=8979; baseline mean age 9.5 years; longitudinal design with baseline, 1-year, and 2-year follow-ups; data collected 2016–2019

FT_IC5.evidence: Published in Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology, 2026; peer-reviewed

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (broad search): Multiple phone/SM-specific numeric estimates found: (a) video streaming, (b) online chatting, (c) TV watching, (d) social media use. Each paired with individual-trait outcome (irritability, measured via parent-rated CBCL). Temporal network analysis reports β coefficients (e.g., online chatting predicts decreased irritability; irritability predicts increased online chatting). Other side is irritability (behavioral/emotion dysregulation outcome, individual-trait level). Criterion met at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Temporal network analysis reports β coefficients for bidirectional associations. Gaussian Graphical Model reports associations. Graphical Vector Autoregressive Model yields β estimates for predictive paths (e.g., 'online chatting predicted decreases in irritability over time')

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results throughout: β coefficients from temporal network analysis; statistical significance testing; results controlled for covariates (ADHD, anxiety, depression)

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies. Paper includes cross-sectional Gaussian Graphical Model analysis with screen variables and irritability as DVs. Per §2.6 (binding): 'if any cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV exists in the paper, EC7 does NOT apply, regardless of how many longitudinal SM-as-DV analyses are present.' Carve-out satisfied; EC7 does not apply.
notes: Bidirectional associations reported between irritability and online chatting; unidirectional associations between TV/social media use and irritability. Paper explicitly controlled for co-occurring psychiatric symptoms. Results highlight emotion dysregulation as an individual-trait outcome linked to specific screen activities.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; n=7043; baseline mean age 9.5 years; longitudinal design with baseline and 2-year follow-up

FT_IC5.evidence: Published in Journal of Adolescence, 2026; peer-reviewed journal

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 (broad search): Multiple phone/SM-specific numeric estimates identified: (a) passive screen watching, (b) social media use, (c) social engagement (digital). Each paired with individual-trait outcomes (cognitive functions: inhibitory control, attention). Linear mixed-effect models report β coefficients for screen time variables predicting cognitive development. Other side is individual-trait level (cognitive/neural development). Criterion met at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect models report β coefficients for associations between screen time variables and cognitive scores. Multiple cognitive outcomes examined (inhibitory control, attention). Sex as moderator also examined with β estimates provided.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results: β coefficients from linear mixed-effect models; analyses of cognitive development trajectories; moderation effects of sex with quantitative estimates; statistical testing of associations

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal with screen time variables as IVs and cognitive development as DV. SM/screen is not exclusively on the DV side (it is the predictor). EC7 applies only when SM is DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analyses. Here, SM/screen is the predictor; therefore EC7 does not apply.
notes: Lifestyle factors (sleep, screen time, physical activity, extracurricular activities) examined as predictors of cognitive development. Screen time components (passive watching, social media, social engagement) show negative associations with cognitive development. Sex moderates some associations, with stronger negative effects of passive watching in females.
